moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Grob Opening, an unconventional first move that immediately stakes a claim on the long diagonal with our bishop-to-be. While it looks unusual, g4 controls the f3 square (preventing Black's knight from settling there easily) and prepares to fianchetto our bishop on g2, where it will exert pressure along the long diagonal toward b1. This opening appeals to players who want to avoid mainstream theory and create immediate imbalance—Black must decide whether to accept the challenge or play solidly.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: g4 controls f3 and fianchettoes the bishop on g2, pressuring the long diagonal toward b1.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black immediately stakes a claim in the center with this pawn advance, following the principle that we shouldn't allow White to control d5 cheaply. The d5 pawn is well-placed and controls critical central squares (c4 and e4), limiting where our pieces can sit comfortably. This is the principled response to 1.g4—Black doesn't need to match White's unconventional approach and instead plays actively in the center.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's d5 pawn controls c4 and e4, limiting White's pieces — the principled response to 1.g4.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Now we complete our fianchetto setup, placing our bishop on the long diagonal where it eyes the important d5 pawn directly and exerts influence down toward a8. Our bishop on g2 works together with potential support from a queen on d1 or pressure from other pieces—the long diagonal becomes a key battleground in this opening. With this move, we've clarified our strategic plan: control the long diagonal and pressure Black's center, while our kingside pawn structure remains flexible for either h4 (attacking play) or f4 (central counterplay) depending on how the position develops.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Bishop on g2 eyes d5 and a8 along the long diagonal, supporting pressure from a queen on d1.
